topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias sat rigidly in the leather armchair, the brittle paper trembling slightly in his hands. The biography, a sprawling, glossy volume detailing his life, lay open on the mahogany desk. Every sentence felt like an accusation. He reread the passage describing his early struggles, the descriptions of his triumphs—they were all meticulously crafted, yet hollowed out of his lived experience. A cold sweat prickled his palms. He cleared his throat, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet study. He had to know who had penned this narrative. A tremor ran through his jaw as he traced a line of text, his breath catching in his chest. The silence of the room amplified the frantic thumping in his ears.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," she whispered, her voice barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system. Clara stared at the printed pages, her fingers gripping the edge of the book so tightly her knuckles were white. The narrative painted a picture of her achievements, a journey she distinctly remembered, but the details—the inflection of her arguments, the very cadence of her thoughts—were alien. A sudden dryness coated her throat. She pushed the book away, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. Her gaze darted around the unfamiliar room, searching for an explanation that didn't exist. She stood up abruptly, needing distance, her movements tight and jerky, as if bracing for impact.

[story 3]

Marcus kept running a hand over his face, the skin feeling clammy under his touch. The biography was a tapestry woven with threads he hadn't spun. He leaned closer to the text, his eyes scanning the meticulously chosen adjectives, searching for a misplaced fact, a slip that would betray the authorship. A shallow intake of air escaped him, followed by a quick, shallow exhale. He began to pace the length of the room, his footsteps echoing too loudly on the polished floor. Each step seemed to amplify the unsettling realization: someone else had curated his entire existence onto these pages, and he had no recourse. He stopped by the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, his shoulders hunched forward.
